response.description_long:
Most engineering teams debug their CI by scrolling raw logs. Pipewright replaces that with structured build telemetry: each step is traced, tagged, and stored, so an engineer can ask 'which test slowed down this week' and get an answer in seconds. The company's thesis is that the build is the least-observed system engineers touch every day, and it builds the missing telemetry layer for CI/CD.

Pipewright's product is a CI/CD observability platform that ingests every pipeline event and turns them into a searchable timeline. A lightweight collector runs as a step in existing pipeline configurations — no agent daemons, no proxy — and streams timing, cache hits, artifact sizes, and test results to a backend with a columnar store keeping 13 months of history. Flake detection replays failing tests in isolation on ephemeral runners and marks nondeterministic ones automatically. Dashboards break down duration by repository, branch, and team, and alert rules fire on regressions against a rolling baseline. Cost attribution meters every job minute against the runner class and rolls costs up by repository, team, and pipeline. For teams running merge queues, Pipewright tracks queue depth, wait time, and eviction causes.

Platform engineering teams at companies between 20 and 2,000 engineers use Pipewright to cut CI spend and keep merge queues moving. Documented use cases range from monorepo build sharding to nightly-release verification. Named customers include Brightloop, a payments infrastructure company that brought its median merge-to-green time from 41 minutes to 14 after resharding its monorepo build on Pipewright's critical-path data; Kestrelworks, a game studio running 400-job nightly builds on self-hosted runners, which uses cost attribution to bill CI back to each game team; and Fernrock Labs, which quarantined 61 flaky tests in its first month and cut its merge queue's eviction rate by half.

Pipewright competes with rolling your own solution from Grafana dashboards and homegrown scripts. Its pitch is that build telemetry is a product, not a side project: the schema, flake detector, and cost attribution come ready, and the collector installs in under ten minutes. The company positions itself against the manual workflow of scrolling raw logs and debugging by anecdote. The founding insight, from ex-infrastructure engineers Dana Whitfield and Arjun Mehta, was that application tracing got good while CI stayed a folder of log files.

Pipewright offers three pricing tiers: Free for open-source projects and teams up to 3 developers (full timeline, 90 days of history); Team at $30 per developer per month (13 months of history, flake detection, cost attribution, merge queue analytics, Slack/PagerDuty alerts); and Enterprise with everything in Team plus SSO, audit logs, self-hosted runner support, and a dedicated Slack channel (annual billing). Every paid plan starts with a 30-day trial.

Founded in 2022 in Seattle, WA, Pipewright is a remote-first team of 14 people concentrated in North American time zones. Integrations cover GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins as first-class citizens; test output parsers cover JUnit XML, Jest, pytest, Go test, and RSpec; alerts route to Slack, PagerDuty, or webhooks; quarantine tickets file to Jira or Linear; and everything is available from a GraphQL API with a Terraform provider for alert rules and budgets as code. Pipewright is SOC 2 Type II certified, and the collector is open source and auditable.
